quiz_questions.question_text: What is impulse?

options.text: The product of force and time: J = FΔt

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'The product of force and time: J = FΔt'. The other options — 'Force divided by time', 'Mass times velocity', and 'Energy times time' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: State the impulse-momentum theorem.
quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'FΔt = Δp'. The other options — 'Ft = mv', 'F = ma', and 'Impulse = KE' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do car airbags reduce injury?

options.text: They extend collision time, reducing average force
They increase the force on the body

quiz_questions.explanation: Applications Car airbags — Extend the collision time → reduce the force on the passenger. The other options — 'They make the car lighter', 'They absorb all the energy', and 'They increase the force on the body' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For a variable force, how do you find impulse?
quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'Area under the F-t graph'. The other options — 'Slope of the F-t graph', 'Peak of the F-t graph', and 'The average time' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can impulse be negative?

options.text: Yes — if the force is in the negative direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'Yes — if the force is in the negative direction'. The other options — 'No, impulse is always positive', 'Only in explosions', and 'Only for light objects' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do you bend your knees when landing a jump?

options.text: It increases stopping time, reducing force

quiz_questions.explanation: Bending your knees increases the stopping time during a landing. Since impulse J = FΔt = Δp (change in momentum is fixed), increasing Δt reduces the average force on your body. The answer is 'It increases stopping time, reducing force'. The other options — 'It makes you heavier', 'It increases the force', and 'It reduces your momentum' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of impulse?
quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse (J) equals the change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp. A larger force or longer contact time produces a greater impulse. The answer is 'N·s (same as kg·m/s)'. The other options — 'J', 'W', and 'Pa·s' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of impulse?
quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse = FΔt has units N·s = kg·m/s, same unit as momentum.

quiz_questions.question_text: How can you increase the impulse on an object?

options.text: Increase the force or increase the contact time

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse=FΔt. Both larger F and longer Δt increase impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does a padded glove reduce the force in a boxing punch?

options.text: It increases contact time, so the same impulse is spread over more time → less peak force

quiz_questions.explanation: Longer Δt for same Δp = smaller average F. Safety principle of padding.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the 'impulse-momentum theorem'?

options.text: The impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J = FΔt = Δp = mΔv

quiz_questions.explanation: J=FΔt=mΔv. Links force/duration of contact to momentum change.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'average force' in an impulse context?

options.text: The constant force that would produce the same impulse over the same time interval
Force at the start of contact

quiz_questions.explanation: F_avg = Δp/Δt = Impulse/Δt. Equivalent steady force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'contact time'?

options.text: The duration over which a force acts during a collision or interaction
Time for an object to stop
Time for one wavelength to pass

quiz_questions.explanation: Δt is the collision interval. Longer contact time → smaller average force for the same impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an 'impulsive force'?

options.text: A large force acting over a very short time that produces a significant momentum change
A constant force applied for 1 second
A force that always doubles momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: Brief but large force (bat, hammer). F×Δt = substantial Δp despite small Δt.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the 'area under a force-time graph'?

options.text: The impulse (and therefore the change in momentum) delivered to the object

quiz_questions.explanation: Area under F-t graph = ∫F dt = impulse = Δp.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a 'soft catch' technique in sports?

options.text: Pulling hands backward increases contact time, reducing peak force for the same impulse

quiz_questions.explanation: By increasing Δt, the same Δp is achieved with smaller F_avg, reducing injury risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'change in momentum' as a vector?

options.text: Δp = p_f − p_i; a vector showing both the magnitude and direction of the momentum change
Magnitude of velocity change times mass, always positive

quiz_questions.explanation: Δp is a vector subtraction: both magnitude and direction matter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a 'rebound' producing larger impulse than stopping?

options.text: When an object bounces back, |Δp| = m(v_i + v_f) > m·v_i (just stopping)

quiz_questions.explanation: Stopping: |Δp|=mv_i. Bouncing back at same speed: |Δp|=2mv_i. Rebound creates larger impulse.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'crumple zones' in cars?

options.text: Structural zones that deform in crashes to increase collision time, reducing peak force on occupants

quiz_questions.explanation: Longer Δt → smaller F_avg for the same Δp (occupant momentum change). Saves lives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the area under an 'acceleration-time graph'?
quiz_questions.explanation: ∫a dt = Δv. Different from F-t area (impulse) and v-t area (displacement).

quiz_questions.question_text: 0.2 kg ball at 15 m/s is caught and stopped in 0.05 s. Average force on catcher?
0.5 kg ball kicked from rest to 20 m/s in 0.1 s. Average force?
0.14 kg baseball (30 m/s east) batted back at 40 m/s west; contact time 0.002 s. Average force?
500 N net force on 50 kg object for 3 s. Change in speed?
1000 kg car changes from 20 to 25 m/s in 2 s. Average net force?
Golf club exerts 2000 N on 0.046 kg ball for 0.0005 s. Ball's final speed?
1 kg ball drops, hits floor at 6 m/s, bounces up at 4 m/s. Impulse from floor?
quiz_questions.explanation: Δp=1×(4−(−6))=10 N·s upward (taking up as positive).

quiz_questions.question_text: 0.3 kg hockey puck moving at 20 m/s stopped in 0.015 s. Average stopping force?
A 0.5 kg ball moving at 8 m/s strikes a wall and rebounds at 6 m/s. Contact time=0.01 s. Average force?
Car crashes: Δp=30,000 N·s. With crumple zone Δt=0.15 s vs rigid car Δt=0.015 s. Force ratio (rigid/crumple)?
quiz_questions.explanation: F_rigid/F_crumple=(Δp/0.015)/(Δp/0.15)=0.15/0.015=10. Crumple zone reduces force by 10×.
